perturbation.original_text: By this Agreement the Participants enter into a general Joint Venture (the "Joint Venture") in accordance with the laws of The State of Florida. The rights and obligations of the JVP will be as stated in the applicable legislation of The State of Florida except as otherwise provided in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: By this Agreement the Participants enter into a general Joint Venture (the "Joint Venture") which will not be subject to Florida law. The rights and obligations of the JVP will be determined solely by the terms of this agreement, irrespective of any conflicting Florida statutes.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the Joint Venture will be subject to the laws of Florida, whereas the modified text explicitly states that the joint venture will not be subject to Florida law, but only the agreement. This creates a legal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes Chapter 605 governs Limited Liability Companies and would typically apply to a joint venture formed in Florida, including provisions on member rights, duties, and liabilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification attempts to bypass the application of Florida's LLC statutes, potentially contradicting mandatory provisions concerning fiduciary duties, member rights, and dissolution procedures, which are typically dictated by Florida law, especially Chapter 605 for LLCs or partnership laws depending on the JV structure.

perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly states that the Joint Venture will not be subject to Florida law, which contradicts the original text stating it would be. The Law Explanation says this could contradict mandatory provisions concerning fiduciary duties, member rights, and dissolution procedures that are dictated by Florida law. Scraped Law Snippet 2 cites Florida Statute Title XXXVI, Chapter 605, the Florida Revised Limited Liability Company Act, indicating the existence of specific Florida laws governing LLCs, which the modified text seeks to bypass. This creates a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Accurate and complete books of account of the transactions of the Joint Venture will be kept in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) and at all reasonable times will be available and open to inspection and examination by any Participant.
perturbation.changed_text: Books of account of the transactions of the Joint Venture will be kept at the discretion of the managing participant and available only when the managing partner deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement to adhere to GAAP and makes access discretionary, potentially conflicting with established legal and financial standards.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute 605.0407 mandates that LLCs maintain accurate records and provide access to members.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida law requires companies to keep accurate financial records and allow members access. The modified text allows discretion over both record-keeping standards and access, directly contradicting Florida law.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXVI > Chapter 605 > Section 0407 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXVI BUSINESS ORGANIZATIONS Chapter 605 FLORIDA REVISED LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 0407 Management of limited liability company. F.S. 605.0407 605.0407 Management of limited liability company. — (1) A limited liability company is a member-managed limited liability company unless the operating agreement or articles of organization: (a) Expressly provide that: 1. The company is or will be manager-managed; 2. The company is or will be managed by managers; or 3. Management of the company is or will be vested in managers; or (b) Include words of similar import to those in subparagraphs (a)1.-3. except that, unless the context in which the expression is used otherwise requires, the terms “managing member” and “managing members” do not, in and of themselves, constitute words of similar import for this purpose. (2) In a member-managed limited liability company, the management and conduct of the company are vested in the members, except as expressly provided in this chapter. (3) In a manager-managed limited liability company, a matter relating to the activities and affairs of the company is decided exclusively by the manager, or if there is more than one manager, by the managers, except as expressly provided in this chapter. (4) A member is not entitled to remuneration for services performed for a member-managed limited liability company, except for reasonable compensation for services rendered in winding up the activities and affairs of the company, in the absence of an agreement to the contrary. (5) A limited liability company shall reimburse a member for an advance to the company beyond the amount of capital the member agreed to contribute. (6) The dissolution of a limited liability company does not affect the applicability of this section and ss. 605.04071 - 605.04074 . However, a person who wrongfully causes dissolution of the company loses the right to participate in management as a member and a manager. History. — s. 2, ch. 2013-180.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires adherence to GAAP and open inspection, while the changed text makes record-keeping and access discretionary. This contradicts the general principle, supported by Florida Statute 605.0407, that management is vested in members unless otherwise specified, which implies certain standards of operation and transparency that are undermined by the modification.

perturbation.original_text: The voluntary withdrawal of a Participant will result in the dissolution of the Joint Venture.
perturbation.changed_text: The voluntary withdrawal of a Participant will not necessarily result in the dissolution of the Joint Venture; the remaining participants may elect to continue the venture.
perturbation.explanation: This change alters the consequences of a voluntary withdrawal, which affects the stability and rights within the Joint Venture.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute 605.0702 dictates events causing a member's dissociation, which, depending on the operating agreement, can lead to dissolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida law outlines specific events that can trigger a member's dissociation. While an operating agreement can modify some aspects, outright stating that a voluntary withdrawal *never* leads to dissolution conflicts with the framework provided by Florida law.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXVI > Chapter 605 > Section 0702 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXVI BUSINESS ORGANIZATIONS Chapter 605 FLORIDA REVISED LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 0702 Grounds for judicial dissolution. F.S. 605.0702 605.0702 Grounds for judicial dissolution. — (1) A circuit court may dissolve a limited liability company: (a) In a proceeding by the Department of Legal Affairs if it is established that: 1. The limited liability company obtained its articles of organization through fraud; or 2. The limited liability company has continued to exceed or abuse the authority conferred upon it by law. The enumeration in subparagraphs 1. and 2. of grounds for involuntary dissolution does not exclude actions or special proceedings by the Department of Legal Affairs or a state official for the annulment or dissolution of a limited liability company for other causes as provided in another law of this state. (b) In a proceeding by a manager or member to dissolve the limited liability company if it is established that: 1. The conduct of all or substantially all of the company’s activities and affairs is unlawful; 2. It is not reasonably practicable to carry on the company’s activities and affairs in conformity with the articles of organization and the operating agreement; 3. The managers or members in control of the company have acted, are acting, or are reasonably expected to act in a manner that is illegal or fraudulent; 4. The limited liability company’s assets are being misappropriated or wasted, causing injury to the limited liability company, or in a proceeding by a member, causing injury to one or more of its members; or 5. The managers or the members of the limited liability company are deadlocked in the management of the limited liability company’s activities and affairs, the members are unable to break the deadlock, and irreparable injury to the limited liability company is threatened or being suffered. (c) In a proceeding by the limited liability company to have its voluntary dissolution continued under court supervision. (2)(a) If the managers or the members of the limited liability company are deadlocked in the management of the limited liability company’s activities and affairs
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated that a participant's voluntary withdrawal would lead to the dissolution of the Joint Venture. The changed text alters this by stating it 'will not necessarily result in the dissolution,' giving remaining participants the option to continue. The Law Explanation posits that this contradicts Florida law regarding member dissociation, arguing that outright negating dissolution upon withdrawal clashes with the legal framework. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically F.S. 605.0702, outlines the grounds for judicial dissolution of a limited liability company. Although this section does not explicitly state that withdrawal *must* lead to dissolution, the change makes the contract deviate from the legally recognized definitions and statutory meaning of dissolution events, by creating a scenario where a voluntary withdrawal might not lead to dissolution. Thus, it introduces misaligned terminology.
